mnemonics.title: RIP Features
mnemonics.content: RIPv2 features: 15 hops max, 30s updates, Distance Vector, uses UDP port 520. Memory tip: R.I.P = RoutIng Protocol with 15 (fifteen) maximum hops - like Rest In Peace at 15.

mnemonics.title: OSPF Features
mnemonics.content: OSPF: Link State, uses Dijkstra, cost-based metric, area-based hierarchy, uses IP protocol 89. Memory: OSPF = Open Shortest Path First - First to find shortest path using O.S.P.F = Open Shortest Path First.

mnemonics.title: Routing Classification
mnemonics.content: Static vs Dynamic: S.T.A.T.I.C = Static Tables Are Totally Inefficient Changing networks. Dynamic adapts automatically.

acronyms.usage: Distance Vector protocol using hop count metric, max 15 hops
Link State protocol using cost metric, hierarchical design
Trustworthiness measure for routing protocols (lower = better)
Routing algorithm type where routers share distance tables with neighbors
Routing algorithm where routers maintain complete network topology map

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with columns: Destination Network, Next Hop, Interface, Metric. Visualize it as a decision-making flowchart at each router.

Imagine a network as a graph with cities as nodes and roads as edges with weights. Start from source city, always pick the closest unvisited city, update distances. Use a whiteboard to mark visited nodes with green, current node with yellow.
Think of it as a rumor mill - each router tells neighbors what it knows, neighbors update their tables, then share again. Draw arrows showing information flow between routers.
Visualize all routers as dancers synchronizing - they start with different information (out of sync), exchange moves (updates), and finally all dance in unison (converged).

analogies.analogy: Routing is like planning a journey using GPS (finding the best route), while forwarding is actually driving the car along that route.
Like asking neighbors for directions - each person only knows distances to nearby places, and you propagate this information gradually.
Like having a complete city map versus only knowing directions from your neighbors. With a full map, you can calculate optimal routes yourself.
Like choosing a route based on distance (fewer turns), time (less traffic), scenic value (highways vs back roads), or toll costs - different priorities give different best routes.
Like all team members in a project updating each other about changes until everyone has the same latest information and agrees on the current status.
Like trusting advice from an expert (low AD) over a beginner (high AD) when both give you directions to the same place.
